narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, you've put real effort into pursuing law school, and I appreciate how challenging it can be to receive this news about your application being rejected. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 57.6% probability of failure—meaning it saw a higher risk of not passing compared to applicants it views as safer bets for success. This prediction directly led to the denial, as the school prioritizes students likely to pass the bar and build strong legal careers. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's mid-range position around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model weaker performance in later undergrad years. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed substantially in the same direction, again falling well below the average, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 34 played the strongest role in pulling the prediction toward passing, though it was still a bit under the dataset average of about 37, showing it helped but couldn't fully offset the other signals. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 similarly nudged things toward success, being close to the typical 3.21 average, while your middle family income bracket and full-time student status provided smaller boosts compared to common profiles. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students because of those low early and late-year deciles, which outweighed your respectable LSAT and GPA in the model's eyes, even as a full-time applicant from a middle-income background like many others. It's tough to hear, especially when you've likely worked hard to get here, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, aiming for a higher LSAT on a retry, and gathering stronger recommendation letters that highlight your growth since those early years. You could also explore post-baccalaureate options or community college bridges to demonstrate upward trends before reapplying. Keep pushing; many students in similar situations turn things around and succeed—your story isn't over yet. We're rooting for you.
